title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
Can you have Multiple Nominees for your Term Insurance Plan?
If you are the primary breadwinner of your family, then one of the concerns that may be troubling you is the future of your family’s finances, especially when you may no longer be around.
Buying a term insurance policy may help relieve you secure the finances of your loved ones in your absence. In the unfortunate event of your passing away, the benefits received from the term insurance plan may help in allowing your loved ones to pursue their dreams without any major financial worries. This may be ensured by appointing the right people as the nominee of your term insurance plan.
However, what if you feel the need to appoint multiple nominees for a term insurance plan, so that each one receives the policy benefits? With most insurance companies, it might be indeed possible to appoint multiple nominees for the same term insurance plan.
Here’s a detailed overview of how such a situation may work.
Who is A Nominee in Term Insurance?
Before we get to the concept of appointing multiple nominees for term insurance plans, let’s get a clearer understanding of who a nominee is and its importance in a term insurance.
A nominee can be an individual the policyholder officially designates to receive the death benefit payout, of their term insurance policy during the policy’s tenure. The policyholder may nominate an individual of their choice as the nominee of their policy.
However, the insurance company and/or the plan may have some terms and conditions as to who may be appointed as the nominee. These terms and conditions should be kept in mind while appointing a nominee or multiple nominees for a term plan.
Usually, one may find the following three categories of nominees in term insurance:
Family members as nominees - To secure the financial future of their dearest ones, policyholders may appoint their spouse, children, or their parents as the nominee. These nominees may be referred to as beneficial nominees. In the event of the policyholder’s demise, the payout is passed on to the beneficial nominee/s.
Non-family nominee - One may also enlist a non-family member, such as a close friend, a business partner, or a distant relative to be the nominee. However, for such a nominee application to be approved, you may have to provide relevant documentation and express your reason for the nomination to the insurer.
Minor nominee - You may also appoint your minor children to be the nominees of your term insurance plan. However, since they are minors and are not be legally allowed to deal with the term insurance payout, you will have to appoint an appointee or custodian. This appointee may receive the claim amount and handle it on behalf of the minor nominees until they come of age.
One might be able to appoint multiple nominees for a term insurance plan within these categories.
Important note: Nomination by policyholder is governed by Section 39 of the Insurance Act, 1938 (‘the Act’) as amended from time to time. The process of appointing a nominee, changing a nominee, and other related aspects shall be carried out as specified in the Act.
Should You Name Multiple Nominees for A Term Insurance Plan?
Appointing a single nominee will have the death benefit payout transferred solely to one individual, as per the terms and conditions of the policy. This may seem like a convenient option.
However, there may be various reasons why appointing multiple nominees may also be considered:
Multiple nominations may help distribute the payout across nominees
When you appoint multiple nominees in a term insurance plan, you may also likely be asked how you may wish to distribute the payout amount amongst all. You may suitably choose a proportion in which you may want each of the nominees to receive the pay-outs.
Thus, having multiple nominees for a term plan may ensure to a certain extent that each party involved, whether it is your dependents or close friends in need, gets their share of the sum assured amount.
Example:
Mr Abhinav bought a term insurance policy with a sum assured of Rs 1 crore. He appointed three nominees - his spouse Vishakha, who was to receive 50% of the claim amount, and both of his parents, who were to receive 25% each.
Mr Abhinav passed away due to an unfortunate accident. Vishakha received Rs 50 lakhs* which she used to supplement the income from her job and send her daughter to a foreign university. Mr Abhinav’s parents, too, received Rs 25 lakhs* each and used the money to fund their medical bills and retirement plans.
*This is for illustrative purposes only. The actual claim amount for this sum assured may vary and can be subjected to standard deductions as per the policy terms and conditions.
Multiple nominations might help in the case of one nominee passing away
Safeguarding loved ones’ futures against the risks to life may be one of the primary reasons many people purchase term insurance. However, what if you appoint a single nominee for your term plan and they, too, go through an unfortunate event and pass away before they are paid their share of the sum assured? In such a case, the heirs or the legal representatives of the nominee will be the recipient of their term insurance sum assured amount, or as dictated as per the provisions of applicable law. [2]
If there are multiple nominees for a term insurance plan then in the event of death of one of the nominees, the benefit amount may be distributed proportionately as per the assigned percentage among the other nominees, provided there is no other conflicting will or legal heir1.
What to Remember When Appointing Multiple Nominees for Term Insurance?
If you are planning to appoint multiple nominees for your term policy, there are a few things you may keep in mind.
Inform all the nominees involved
Apart from letting your insurer know that you wish to have multiple nominees, you may also want to inform the nominated persons about your plans. Make sure they have the required information about your policy and know how to raise a claim. Another important thing to ensure is that each of the nominees is aware of the percentage of the sum assured they may be eligible to receive.
Notify the insurer in case of the nominee’s passing away
If you have enlisted multiple nominees for your term insurance plan, and one of the enlisted nominees passes away during the policy tenure, be sure to notify the insurer about the same. The unavailability of one nominee may mean that the original distribution of the sum assured may have to be reallocated. It may be preferred to carry out this procedure at the earliest.
You may change nominees during the policy tenure
You may have the liberty to change the nominee/s of the term insurance plan via an endorsement on the plan. [3] To add a nominee, you may have to fill out a nomination form and provide the relevant information and documentation to the insurer. The sum assured might have to be reallocated as well. After this activity, you may have to ensure that you inform the other nominees about the same.
One may also note that the premium or the cost of the policy may not get affected by a change in the nominee. Neither might it be influenced by the addition or removal of a nominee.
Regardless, if you want to get an estimate of what the premiums of your term insurance plan may be based on the coverage you want, you may take the help of a term insurance calculator. It might provide you with an estimate of the premium you may have to pay based on the variables you enter, such as age, gender, tenure, and so on.
Conclusion
To sum it up, one may have multiple nominees for their term insurance plan.  The term insurance sum assured may have to be distributed to the different nominees as decided by the policyholder during the policy tenure.
When choosing multiple nominees, it is crucial to inform the insurer as well as the nominees if any changes have been made to the prevailing situation.
Source:
1https://www.financialexpress.com/money/insurance/should-you-have-more-than-one-nominee-in-your-term-insurance/2528997/
2https://www.indiacode.nic.in/show-data?actid=AC_CEN_2_33_00044_193804_1523351752525&orderno=74#:~:text=Subject%20as%20aforesaid%2C
3https://www.indiacode.nic.in/show-data?actid=AC_CEN_2_33_00044_193804_1523351752525&orderno=74#:~:text=(2)-,Any%20such%20nomination,-in%20order%20to

https://www.bajajfinservmarkets.in/insurance/term-insurance-plans/articles/term-insurance-nominee.html
https://www.bajajallianzlife.com/life-insurance-guide/term/term-insurance-nomination-related-faqs.html

 Family members as nominees - To secure the financial future of their dearest ones,
policyholders may appoint their spouse, children, or their parents as the nominee. These nominees may be referred to as beneficial nominees. In the event of the policyholder’s demise, the payout is passed on to the beneficial nominee/s.

Appointing a single nominee will have the death benefit payout transferred solely to one individual, as per the terms and conditions of the policy. This may seem like a convenient option. The actual claim amount for this sum assured may vary and can be subjected to standard deductions as per the policy terms and conditions.

[2] If there are multiple nominees for a term insurance plan then in the event of death of one of the nominees, the benefit amount may be distributed proportionately as per the assigned percentage among the other nominees, provided there is no other conflicting will or legal heir1. Inform all the nominees involved
